title: Listening - Part 4: Working as a Park Ranger
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Tom, who works as a national park ranger. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest this afternoon is Tom Evans, a ranger in a national park. Tom, did you grow up wanting to work outdoors? || Tom: I enjoyed camping, but I originally trained as a mechanic and repaired buses for several years. I volunteered one weekend to clear rubbish from a walking trail after a busy holiday period. [24]Seeing how quickly an attractive natural place could be damaged by careless visitors made me want to help protect it permanently[/24]. I studied countryside management part-time, then applied for ranger posts. || Presenter: What do visitors misunderstand about a ranger’s work? || Tom: Many imagine I spend the whole day watching deer through binoculars. Wildlife monitoring is part of the job, but there’s much more. [25]Most weeks involve maintaining paths, answering visitors’ questions and checking that signs and gates are safe[/25]. It is practical work with a lot of contact with people, which suits me. || Presenter: Are visitors usually well behaved? || Tom: Most are considerate and want to do the right thing. Problems often arise from lack of information rather than deliberate harm. For instance, a family may let a dog run freely without realising birds are nesting on the ground nearby. [26]I find explaining the reason for a rule is generally more effective than simply telling someone they have broken it[/26]. People cooperate when they understand the consequence. || Presenter: Have you faced any dangerous situations? || Tom: Weather causes more difficulty than animals do. During one winter afternoon, snow arrived faster than forecast and several walkers failed to return to the car park. [27]Using the route information they had left with a friend, our team located them before darkness and guided them back safely[/27]. It showed me how valuable simple preparation can be. || Presenter: Has the park changed since you began? || Tom: Visitor numbers have increased, especially after people discovered local walks during recent years. We want people to enjoy the park, but popular paths were becoming worn away. [28]We have added raised wooden sections on certain routes so that walkers can pass without damaging wet ground underneath[/28]. The change protects habitats while keeping access open. || Presenter: Do you enjoy working with volunteers? || Tom: Very much. Volunteers help with surveys and repairs, and they bring different knowledge. [29]One retired teacher created nature activities for families, which made our guided walks much more engaging for children[/29]. Staff could not provide everything alone. || Presenter: What should people do before a long walk in a park? || Tom: They should check weather forecasts and carry suitable clothing, even on a fine morning. Most importantly, [30]tell somebody which route you intend to follow and when you expect to return[/30]. If something goes wrong, that information can save valuable time.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Tom decide to become a park ranger?

options.A: He wanted a job that used his mechanical training.
options.B: He became concerned about harm caused to a natural area.
options.C: He was offered ranger training while volunteering.

questions.explanation: While clearing rubbish, he saw how visitors could damage a natural place.

questions.question: What does Tom say about his normal work?

options.A: It is mostly concerned with observing wildlife.
options.B: It involves practical tasks and helping park users.
options.C: It requires him to spend little time outdoors.

questions.explanation: He mentions path maintenance, visitor questions and safety checks.

questions.question: How does Tom usually deal with visitors who do something wrong?

options.A: He tells them why a restriction is necessary.
options.C: He asks other visitors to speak to them.

questions.explanation: Tom finds explanation more effective than merely saying a rule has been broken.

questions.question: How were lost walkers found during a snowstorm?

options.A: Their dog led rangers to their position.
options.B: They managed to contact the car park directly.
options.C: Rangers used information about their planned route.

questions.explanation: The team located them by using route information left with a friend.

questions.question: Why were wooden sections added to some paths?

options.A: To help protect the ground from large numbers of walkers.
options.B: To provide better views for wildlife observation.
options.C: To enable rangers to use vehicles in wet weather.

questions.explanation: Raised paths let visitors pass over wet ground without damaging it.

questions.question: What example does Tom give of volunteers' value?

options.B: A volunteer improved activities for younger visitors.
options.C: They persuaded more people to follow the rules.

questions.explanation: A retired teacher designed nature activities that engaged children.

questions.question: What does Tom particularly recommend before a long walk?

options.A: Checking whether guided walks are available.
options.C: Informing someone of your route and return time.

questions.explanation: He stresses that route and expected return details can help rescuers.
